ABSTRACT

Achieving thermodynamic faithfulness and transferability across state points is an outstanding challenge in the bottom-up coarse graining of molecular models, with many efforts focusing on augmenting the form of coarse-grained interaction potentials to improve transferability. Here, we revisit the critical role of the simulation ensemble and the possibility that even simple models can be made more predictive through a smarter choice of ensemble. We highlight the efficacy of coarse graining from ensembles where variables conjugate to the thermodynamic quantities of interest are forced to respond to applied perturbations. For example, to learn activity coefficients, it is natural to coarse grain from ensembles with spatially varying external potentials applied to one species to force local composition variations and fluctuations. We apply this strategy to coarse grain both an atomistic model of water and methanol and a binary mixture of spheres interacting via Gaussian repulsions and demonstrate near-quantitative capture of activity coefficients across the whole composition range. Furthermore, the approach is able to do so without explicitly measuring and targeting activity coefficients during the coarse graining process; activity coefficients are only computed after-the-fact to assess accuracy. We hypothesize that ensembles with applied thermodynamic potentials are more “thermodynamically informative.” We quantify this notion of informativeness using the Fisher information metric, which enables the systematic design of optimal bias potentials that promote the learning of thermodynamically faithful models. The Fisher information is related to variances of structural variables, highlighting the physical basis underlying the Fisher information’s utility in improving coarse-grained models. I. INTRODUCTION An outstanding problem in the field of bottom-up coarse graining is the faithful preservation of thermodynamic properties from a reference system, typically atomically detailed, to a

J. Chem. Phys. 153, 154116 (2020); doi: 10.1063/5.0022808 Published under license by AIP Publishing

coarse-grained model that has fewer degrees of freedom, simpler functional forms for the interaction potential, or both.1–6 There are several promising frameworks for effecting the coarse-grain parameterization, with Boltzmann inversion (based on matching structural properties),7–9 multi-scale coarse graining (based on matching

[Page 3]

force residuals),1–3,10–23 and relative entropy (based on minimizing information loss)14,24–30 being among the most common systematic frameworks. More recently, approaches based on newer machine learning methods have also emerged.31–36 However, it has been well-documented that while excelling at reproducing quantities such as the pair correlation function g(r),7,8,37 without special treatment, these methods often produce coarse-grained models that predict very different thermodynamic behaviors than those of the reference systems from which the models were parameterized.4,38 For example, in one coarse-graining study of benzene–water mixtures, the coarse-grained model failed to phase separate where the atomistic model correctly predicted phase separation.39 There have also been numerous studies indicating that coarse-grained models often over-predict the pressure of the reference system, sometimes by over a factor of 1000.8,18 The most common remedy to this thermodynamic “lossiness” is to measure and incorporate the target thermodynamic quantity into the optimization objective. For example, pressure-matching schemes measure the pressure of both reference and coarse-grained models and seek to minimize the difference.7,8,18,40 Promising work has also been done matching Kirkwood–Buff integrals.41–43 Still, thermodynamic quantities that have not been targeted are not guaranteed to be reproduced.44 Generally, extensive validation is required to ensure confidence in the faithful preservation of thermodynamic behavior from the reference to the coarse-grained system. This state of affairs hinders the development of new coarsegrained models based on increasingly accurate atomistic and firstprinciples models.45–47 Most severely, there are few techniques41–43 to ensure that coarse-grained models reproduce thermodynamic quantities that are themselves difficult to measure, such as the chemical potential. If a bottom-up coarse-grained model gives unrealistic thermodynamic predictions, it is usually not clear if the atomistic reference or the coarse-grained parameterization is at fault. Thus, bottom-up coarse graining is often supplemented with top-down approaches48–51 that draw upon available experimental measurements to further constrain the bottom-up coarse-grained model.52–54 One approach to improving coarse-grained models is to use more elaborate functional forms for the interaction potentials. Much work has been put into this area, notably state-dependent extended Hamiltonians to improve transferability,18,40 and the development of local density functionals, which are multibody in nature.22,23,28,29,55–57 Tractable coarse-grained potentials are, by necessity, much simpler than the full multibody potential of mean force and must balance the simultaneous reproduction of multiple properties of the detailed reference system. The underlying rationale behind using more elaborate interactions is that they should be able to better approximate the underlying potential of mean force and thus reproduce more properties simultaneously. In this work, we tackle the problem from a complementary perspective—given a specified functional form of the coarsegrained potentials, how can a bottom-up approach be designed to find parameters that best reproduce macroscopic thermodynamic properties? This line of inquiry is motivated by the following observations: first, many successful coarse-grained models, such as dissipative particle dynamics (DPD),58 MARTINI,59,60 and SAFT-Mie,50,51 use very simple functional forms but can still reproduce many macroscopic thermodynamic properties, suggesting that
J. Chem. Phys. 153, 154116 (2020); doi: 10.1063/5.0022808 Published under license by AIP Publishing

simple functional forms can be sufficiently thermodynamically expressive.61 Second, many bottom-up coarse-graining studies use fully flexible pair-potentials in the form of tabulated or spline potentials, yet nevertheless fail to reproduce thermodynamic behavior.8 It is thus clear that much opportunity lies in improving the coarsegraining procedure itself, which is complementary to advances in functional forms. For the coarse-graining methodology and analysis, we rely on minimizing the relative entropy,24 also known as the Kullback– Leibler divergence,62 which is a rigorous definition of information loss between two probability distributions. The relative entropy allows us to critically examine what information is being learned and retained by a coarse-grained model. We first demonstrate that standard applications of relative entropy coarse graining in the NVT ensemble of a binary mixture can yield qualitatively incorrect coarsegrained models: ideal mixtures can be coarse grained to non-ideal mixtures and miscible systems may even be coarse grained to immiscible models. This coarse-graining behavior is consistent with frequent observations that coarse graining using structure-matching and force-matching approaches in the NVT ensemble significantly overestimate the pressure7,8,63 or fail to reproduce solution miscibility.39 In those alternative coarse-graining methodologies, this failure to capture thermodynamics is perhaps not too surprising— requiring structure matching and force matching to reproduce thermodynamic quantities is asking them to learn quantities that were never measured or optimized for. However, in the context of relative entropy minimization, this failure to reproduce thermodynamics might seem a bit more puzzling: If we have formally minimized information loss, what else can the coarse-grained model learn? We propose that the solution lies in re-examining the role of the simulation ensemble employed in the coarse-graining process. The ensemble is, after all, our prescription for how to collect a trajectory of molecular configurations (i.e., the “information” we will coarse grain or “learn” from). A logical way to improve upon single-state coarse graining is to try learning from simulations at multiple state points. This state-of-the-art approach is known as coarse graining using “multi” or “extended” ensembles17,19,30,49,64 and is performed by averaging the coarse-graining errors at each state point using a pre-determined weighting. We more precisely term this approach fixed-weight extended ensemble coarse graining and demonstrate that not only do fixed-weight extended ensembles not remedy the coarse-graining problem for the systems we consider, they can even decrease thermodynamic faithfulness. Instead, a more natural way to achieve thermodynamically accurate coarse graining is to make sure that the desired behavior is encoded in the simulated ensemble in an accessible way. This idea is founded on the simple observation that the thermodynamic properties of interest (e.g., pressure, surface tension, and chemical potential) have conjugate variables that are often easier to measure than the thermodynamic property itself. Correspondingly, by simulating an ensemble with an applied thermodynamic force, the response of the conjugate variable gives a readily measurable assessment of whether the underlying thermodynamics are reproduced. Consider the aforementioned examples where the NVT-coarsegrained models predict widely incorrect pressures.7,8,40 To improve the reproduction of the pressure, we argue that a pressureinformative ensemble is the one that allows for volume changes.
[Page 4]

While NVT trajectories do contain pressure information (i.e., one can evaluate the virial expression), this pressure information is apparently not directly learnable by structure matching, force matching, or relative entropy minimization on coarse-grained functional forms; instead, one needs to explicitly process the trajectory to measure the pressure and supply this information to coarse-graining algorithms for learning.7,40 In contrast, in the NPT ensemble, incorrect coarse-grained model pressures would result in incorrect volume changes that manifest readily in the structure, without the need for additional measurements. Recently, there have been observations that coarse graining in the NPT ensemble can improve coarsegrained models,37 and iterative simulations of the coarse-grained model in the NPT ensemble have been used to assess and refine the pressure–volume response of the coarse-grained models derived from force-matching approaches.18 Next, consider binary mixtures for which an important quantity is the activity coefficient. Measuring the activity coefficient requires calculating the chemical potential at both the state of interest and a reference state, which further complicates endeavors to match it by augmenting the objective, as done in pressurematching schemes. With the ensemble approach, we first identify the conjugate variable as the mixture composition, which can be probed by applying a bias between two simulation boxes that are allowed to exchange particles or by applying an inhomogeneous exchange potential to a single simulation box. In such an external-potential ensemble, incorrect coarse-grained model activities will result in incorrect composition responses, which are again readily detected and learned by relative entropy optimization. This approach is consistent with observations in the literature that naturally inhomogeneous systems, such as phase-separated systems, can yield coarse-grained models with improved thermodynamic faithfulness.29,57,65 Furthermore, we demonstrate that such ensembles can be systematically designed and optimized. To quantify the intuition that it is more informative to coarse grain from ensembles probing a system’s response to thermodynamic forces, we use the second parametric derivative of the relative entropy, which is closely related to the Fisher information.66 We show that maximizing the Fisher information as a function of the external-potential strength leads to coarse-grained models that can near-quantitatively reproduce the activity coefficients of the reference system, across the entire composition range, despite never directly targeting the activity coefficient during model optimization. We apply this approach by coarse graining both a binary mixture of Gaussian repulsive spheres and an atomistic model of methanol–water mixtures. While there have been reports in the literature performing bottom-up coarse graining of mixtures,19,20,39,57,67,68 including coarse-graining studies on methanol and water,20,56,63 most reports largely focus on fidelity with respect to the liquid structure and density, with only a few examining solvation energies49,63 and derivatives of the activity coefficient.41,42 The present work contributes to the nascent literature on reproducing the mixture activity coefficients with a bottom-up coarse graining approach. In Sec. II, we describe our model systems, simulation methods, and how we apply the Fisher information to analyze coarse-grained models. In Sec. III, we show simulation results validating the idea of coarse graining from ensembles that probe composition responses. Finally, we discuss the generality and implications of the approach.

II. COMPUTATIONAL DETAILS AND THEORY A. Reference systems and coarse-grained models We consider the coarse graining of two different reference systems (Fig. 1). The first is an atomistically resolved mixture of water and methanol. For water, we use the OPC force field,69 while for methanol, we use the GAFF-2 force field.70 The charges on the methanol molecule are chosen using the AM1-BCC empirical charging method and subsequently rescaled as suggested by Fennell et al. to account for the polarization of carbonyl and hydroxyl groups in hydrogen-bond-rich environments.71 Although Fennell et al. originally applied the method to the GAFF force field, we find that their charge-scaling prescription alone, without modification, yields excellent results for dielectric constants when applied to the GAFF-2 force field for small alcohols, as detailed in Appendix A. The thermodynamic properties are calculated at T = 298 K and P = 1 atm. The self-repulsion parameters are chosen as uAA = uBB = 20v 0 , and the mismatch parameter Δu = uAB − 12 (uAA + uBB ) controls the degree of incompatibility between the two species. Mixing free energies are evaluated in the NPT ensemble and set to a reference pressure βP = 10.31/v 0 that was a priori determined by measuring the pressure of a pure-species system held at density ρ = 1/v 0 . If v 0 is equated with the molecular volume of water, v w , this reference system has a compressibility of ≈0.05v w /kB T, which is comparable to that of water at room temperature 4.5 ⋅ 10−10 Pa−1 ≈ 0.062v w /kB T. An important aspect of both reference systems is that they exhibit pronounced liquid structure, with a well-developed correlation hole and oscillations in the pair distribution function. To effect the coarse graining, we map the reference systems to highly softened single-site models that average over the liquid structure and permit molecules to overlap. This form of coarse graining is motivated by the desire to eliminate short-length-scale features that are computationally expensive to resolve. This approach is common in the literature,58,77 most notably in the soft-core models that are used in dissipative particle dynamics models.54,58 Applying this approach to the symmetric binary mixture, the reference system with bead radii aref = (2π)−1/2 l0 ≈ 0.398l0 is coarse grained to a system with acg = 1l0 . Increasing the length scale of the interaction means that, at the same pressure and density, particles overlap more, which reduces the magnitude of correlation holes and oscillations in the pair distribution function. The smearing length might seem small, but similar values of the coarse-grained smearing length acg ≳ l0 have been reported in the literature, e.g., if one were to map the dimensionless phase diagrams of Gaussian-repulsive block copolymers to a real system such as polystyrene-b-polyisoprene at T = 373 K.73 Verification that the coarse-grained models used here do indeed average over the liquid structure as claimed is presented in Sec. III. The atomistic methanol–water mixture is similarly coarse grained to a mixture of asymmetric Gaussian beads located at the centers-of-mass of the methanol and water molecules. Although this coarse-grained model may seem rudimentary (e.g., it lacks any electrostatic interaction, and molecules become rotationally isotropic), we demonstrate that a careful coarse-graining procedure permits it to express realistic mixing thermodynamics of a methanol–water mixture. In this case, the unit volume is chosen to be equal to the water molecular volume v w = 0.0300 nm3 (determined from the simulations of the OPC water model at T = 298 K, P = 1 atm), which, 1/3 in turn, sets the unit length lw = vw = 0.3107 nm. The coarsegrained interaction radii are chosen as amoh = aw = lw . Although methanol has roughly twice the molecular volume of water, for the soft coarse-grained interactions and liquid densities used in this work, setting amoh = aw is still large enough such that there is a significant overlap between methanol molecules. In this limit of an appreciable bead overlap, it is instead the unequal self-repulsion parameters uww and umoh – moh that determine the effective molecular volumes at the specified pressure. The most notable and unconventional aspect of this joint distribution is that, by construction, the probability of observing a given state γ is fixed by the weights wγ and is thus independent of the underlying coarse-grained potential. This property simplifies the process of simulating the reference system for the purposes of collecting trajectory data for coarse graining: one simply needs to run each sub-ensemble γ and sum their respective relative entropies. However, this clearly stands in contrast to the more traditional ensembles such as the NPT or μVT ensembles, where the relative likelihood of observing a macrostate γ depends on both its canonical partition function (and hence the coarse-grained model) and an additional exponential factor. To capture mixture thermodynamics, a more natural ensemble to consider is a kind of semi-grand ensemble, where particles are allowed to exchange between two boxes with fixed volumes. This ensemble can be augmented by applying an external potential biasing one of the species to favor one of the two boxes. In contrast to the fixed-weight extended ensemble, the probability weight of a given macrostate γ = {NAI , NBI } now depends on the partition function of the macrostate, which is, in turn, determined by the interaction potential. By applying non-zero values of the external potential (U ext ≠ 0), one can probe the system’s response and thermodynamic behavior by exploiting the fact that the degree of segregation is dependent both on U ext and the intrinsic mixing incompatibility of the two species. To see this, consider two equal-volume boxes, a symmetric, incompressible mixture described by a single Margules parameter A12 , and modulating the partitioning by applying an external potential with strength U ext = 1kB T to species A. If the mixture were ideal, i.e., A12 = 0, the equilibrium partitioning is approximately xAI ≈ 0.27, xAII ≈ 0.73. In comparison, if the mixture is non-ideal with A12 = 1, applying U ext = 1kB T leads to an equilibrium of roughly xAI ≈ 0.15, xAII ≈ 0.85. Clearly, applying an external potential enables the ensemble to distinguish between two solutions with differing degrees of non-ideality. While this approach is illustrative for pedagogical purposes, implementing the semi-grand ensemble in simulations requires

[Page 7]

molecule insertion and deletion steps that are computationally expensive to perform for large or complex molecules. To circumvent this difficulty, we note that the semi-grand ensemble described above is the limit of simulating one large simulation box with a square wave potential. In practice, we implement a finite-size approximation of the semi-grand ensemble by simulating a single long simulation box with a sinusoidal external potential U(x) = U ext sin(2πnp x/L) applied to one of the species, where U ext is the external potential strength, np is the number of periods, and L is the length of the box in the x-direction; we call this the “external-potential ensemble.” By construction, this ensemble is very efficient to simulate—only a single, straightforward simulation is required, with no need for insertions or deletions. C. Optimizing the ensemble information content In Sec. II B, we introduced the idea of using an externalpotential ensemble to improve the coarse graining of mixture thermodynamics. However, the type of external potential to apply to the system has not been specified. We propose that the Hessian of the relative entropy with respect to interaction parameters should play a fundamental role in selecting an appropriate ensemble. The Hessian naturally arises as part of the numerical procedure of minimizing the relative entropy.79 Beyond its utility in the optimization procedure, in parametric statistics, the Hessian of the relative entropy is also a useful quantity known as the Fisher information matrix I, Iij (λ̂) =

The Fisher information matrix is known to control the distribution of λ̂ estimated from minimizing the relative entropy: the probabilistic nature of the estimated parameters arises from using finite-sampling to estimate the reference and coarse-grained probability distributions. Specifically, the distribution of a vector λ̂ of parameter estimators about the true relative entropy minimum λ̂0 asymptotically follows a normal distribution,66 √ n(λ̂ − λ̂0 ) → N(0, I −1 (λ̂0 )), (2.14)

We propose that the external-potential ensemble can be optimized by maximizing the Fisher information. In this work, in which we focus on optimizing only the cross-interaction parameter, the Fisher information matrix reduces to a scalar and the maximum is well defined; in a general setting, one can envision maximizing components or the determinant of the Fisher information matrix. There are strong physical reasons suggesting that maximizing the Fisher information might correlate with the improved thermodynamic predictions of coarse-grained models. In particular, consider the case where the coarse-grained interaction potentials have the form ∑i λi X i (R), where X i is a structural variable dependent on the coarse-grained configuration R. Common interaction potentials such as Lennard-Jones interactions, spline potentials, and tabulated potentials can be written in this linear form. In this work, we focus on maximizing the Fisher information of the A–B interaction. This corresponds to maximizing the variance of the total number of A–B contacts or, alternatively, maximizing the variance of the sum of local concentrations of species B about species A. It is physically reasonable that increasing the variance of the structural variables, which would correspond to a wider (and flatter) sampling of possible values, correlates with the improved learning outcomes and thermodynamic predictions. In Sec. III, we present the results confirming this hypothesis. D. Simulation protocols We simulate mixtures of particles interacting via Gaussians (including the coarse-grained water–methanol system) in dimensionless units at T = 1 using a Langevin thermostat with a damping relaxation √ time of 1τ and an integrator time step dt = 0.01τ, where τ = ε/ml02 is the unit of time. Reference system trajectories are gathered from a variety of box sizes, with the longest box dimension ranging from L = 10l0 to L = 60l0 to probe finite-size effects. Both cubic and square cuboid boxes are considered. External potential calculations are carried out by first equilibrating a simulation box at constant pressure without the external potential, before fixing the box at the average volume and subsequently turning on the external potential. The chemical potentials of mixtures of Gaussian particles are calculated by first equilibrating in the NPT ensemble for 10 000τ, before simulating the model at the prescribed mole fraction in the NPT ensemble for 40 000τ. Next, 100 Widom insertions per frame are performed to calculate the excess chemical potential. As explained in Sec. III, for NPT simulations, the reference binary Gaussian mixture and coarse-grained models derived from it are simulated at Pref = 10.31kB T/v 0 , while the coarse-grained methanol– water mixture is simulated at Pref = 8.52kB T/v w , a pressure chosen such that the coarse-grained model’s compressibility is comparable to that of the atomistic water model. All atomistic water–methanol simulations are conducted at T = 298 K and P = 1 bar. Reference external potential calculations are conducted by applying the external potential to a simulation box that is roughly 50/50 volume fraction water and methanol, estimated using the pure-state molecular volumes. The simulation box is initialized to 4.66 × 4.66 × 9.32 nm3 , and the box length is relaxed via a NPn AT simulation (holding the x–y dimensions and hence the cross-sectional area fixed) before applying the external potential. Simulations are equilibrated for 10 ns and 50 ns before collecting trajectory data over 50 ns and 200 ns for external potential strengths U ext = 0.0 and U ext > 0.0, respectively. The chemical potentials of the atomistic water–methanol reference system are calculated by the multistate Bennet acceptance ratio (MBAR) technique84 while staging the deletion of a molecule over

31 stages, as has been suggested in the literature:85 the charge interaction between the deleted molecule and the rest of the molecules is scaled down by a factor λQ = [1.0, 0.95, . . ., 0.05, 0.0] over evenly spaced stages, and subsequently, the Lennard-Jones interaction is scaled down by a factor of λLJ = [1.0, 0.9, . . ., 0.1, 0.0]. Each stage is equilibrated for 10 ns before subsequently collecting trajectory data over 10 ns. All simulations are conducted using the OpenMM simulation package.86 Meanwhile, minimization of the relative entropy is carried out using a conjugate gradient descent scheme outlined previously.25,28 To accelerate the minimization procedure and mitigate the stochasticity of sampling coarse-grained trajectories during parameterization, we use an efficient trajectory reweighting and perturbation strategy.25 III. The first step is to coarse grain the self-interactions from single-species simulations. Choosing a pure-state particle density ρ = 1/v 0 and a reference selfinteraction uref ,AA = 20v 0 yields a pressure of Pref = 10.31kB T/v 0 in the reference system. Following past studies, coarse graining is first investigated in the NVT ensemble.79 As desired, by increasing the interaction length scale from aref = (2π)−1/2 l0 in the reference system to acg = 1l0 in the coarse-grained system, the NVT coarse-grained model significantly reduces liquid structuring. In Fig. 3, this manifests as a significant decrease in the peak of the pair distribution function, in addition to an appreciable particle overlap. However, we also find that the coarse-grained model severely overestimates

To better reproduce the pressure, we follow our hypothesized prescription of coarse graining in ensembles where variables conjugate to the thermodynamic property of interest are allowed to fluctuate; to reproduce the pressure, this means we allow volume fluctuations and coarse grain in the NPT ensemble with the pressure held at Pref = 10.31kT/v 0 in both the reference and coarse-grained ensembles. Doing so produces a coarse-grained model that further reduces the liquid structure observed (Fig. 3); the like–like interaction parameter is a much more reasonable ucg ,AA = 18.93v 0 and reproduces the liquid density to within 0.6% when simulated at Pref = 10.31kT/v 0 . The success of coarse graining in the NPT ensemble is consistent with our idea of thermodynamically informative ensembles, but it also relies on measuring and setting a target pressure; whereas the pressure is relatively easy to measure, activity coefficients are difficult to measure accurately for atomistic systems and represent a significantly more challenging target. Thus, after fixing the selfinteraction parameters of the pure components by coarse graining using the NPT ensemble as described above, we now direct our attention to the optimization of the cross-interaction parameter uAB . For a symmetric mixture with uAA = uBB and xA = xB = 0.5, it is helpful to consider the mismatch Δu = uAB − 21 (uAA + uBB ). When Δu = 0.0, the mixture is ideal; deviations from zero measure the non-ideality of the system. For reference systems with non-zero mismatch parameters, we initialize simulation boxes at an overall bead density of ρ = 1/v 0 and allow the simulation box to relax in the NPAT ensemble at the reference pressure Pref = 10.31kB T/v 0 , before running NVT ensembles at the equilibrium box sizes. Due to the low compressibilities of the system, box dimensions only change by 1%–2%, so in the text, we refer to systems by their initial box dimensions instead of the relaxed dimensions (e.g., L = 50l0 instead of L = 50.33l0 ).

We first consider the NVT ensemble, the most common coarsegraining ensemble. We present coarse-graining results of two different miscible reference systems, with the mismatch parameter Δuref = 0.0, 2.0, respectively. Since the coarse-grained interactions have longer characteristic length scales, we need to be conscious of finitesize effects. As the ratio of the box size to the interaction length scale decreases, longer wavelength fluctuations will be artificially suppressed, thus biasing the sampled distribution of molecular configurations. To explore this issue, in Fig. 4(a), we present the results from coarse graining equimolar mixtures in cubic boxes as a function of inverse box sizes 1/L. We see that the coarse-grained mismatch parameter Δucg decreases with the increasing box size. As the box size decreases, the mismatch parameter seems to increase to partially compensate for fluctuations now suppressed by the finite box size. A stark example of the effect of coarse-graining ensemble can be seen for the case where we coarse grain a reference fluid with Δuref = 2.0 using a box size of length L = 10l0 . In this case, the coarse-grained mismatch parameter is Δucg = 2.59. While this coarse-grained model is by inspection still miscible in the small box L = 10l0 , when the same model is simulated at larger box sizes, we see a significant amount of segregation between the two species [Fig. 4(b)]. This is an extreme example of how the coarse-graining process may be unable to preserve even qualitatively the macroscopic thermodynamic behavior of the reference system. Here, the main culprit lies in the conditions under which we simulate and coarse grain the reference fluid: a box size of L = 10l0 is too small. These finite-size artifacts are easily mitigated by increasing the box size. As demonstrated in Fig. 4(a), there is a clear limit to the variables as 1/L → 0. In practice, we recognize that significant computational savings can be achieved by the use of rectangular boxes instead of cubic boxes. For reference, we also show the Fisher information of the extended ensemble system in Fig. 5(a) on the same per-particle basis. Due to the assumption of fixed equal weights, the Fisher information [Eq. (2.16)] is simply a weighted average of the Fisher information of the individual subsystems, each of which is a single-state NVT system with low information content per particle. Although the full extended ensemble uses trajectory data at multiple state points, the extended ensemble nevertheless has a lower information content on a per-particle basis than even a single NVT simulation at an equimolar composition; the ostensible variety of configurations does not translate into a useful increase of information that the coarse-grained model can learn from. We note that far away from the Fisher information maximum, the correlation between Fisher information and thermodynamic faithfulness is not necessarily monotonic. For the non-ideal mixture, the U ext = 0.0 ensemble has a slightly higher Fisher information than the U ext = 4.0 ensemble yet produces markedly less faithful activity coefficients than the latter system. Meanwhile, for the ideal mixture, the Fisher information of the U ext = 0.0 ensemble indeed has the lowest value (near zero) and correspondingly yields the worst (most non-ideal) predictions of the cross-interaction. That said, in both cases, the value of the Fisher information maximum is that much higher than the corresponding value in the U ext = 0.0 ensemble such that we expect the maximum of the Fisher information is still a reliable metric for the thermodynamic faithfulness of the model. This maximum can, in turn, be understood by examining the statistics of the effective local concentrations N AB [Eq. (2.18)] of species B about a particle of species A, as presented in Fig. 5(c). The local concentrations about two different particles i and j of species A are correlated, and the Fisher information is the sum ∑ij σij2 [Eq. (2.20)] over all the variances σii2 and covariances σi≠j of the local concentrations N AB,i and N AB,j about particles i and j of species A. It is important to appreciate that introducing the external potential monotonically decreases the average (and total) number of A–B contacts, as summarized by ⟨NAB ⟩ in Fig. 5(c). As the external potential strength is increased, the increasing segregation of the two species decreases the number of contacts and confines them to the region of a sharp interface. This is expected to decrease the total variance and Fisher information. What, then, drives the initial increase in the Fisher information with the increasing external potential strength? A natural first guess is that the external potential broadens the distribution of compositions in the box and hence the variance σii2 of N AB [example probability distributions P(N AB ) given in Appendix B]. The diagonal term N1A ∑i σii2 isolates the contribution to the total variance driven solely by the broadening of the marginal distribution of local densities: if particle local densities are independently drawn from the marginal, this would be the only term that contributes. In Fig. 5(c), N1A ∑i σii2 initially increases with the increasing external potential strengths, confirming expectations. However, at intermediate values of U ext , the diagonal term is only a fraction of the total variance, it shows only a weak maximum, and its peak does not coincide with the peak of the total variance. Instead, the peak in the total variance more closely follows the peak in the covariance contributions σij2 . For low to intermediate external potential strengths, the primary contribution to the total variance is due to the correlations in the local densities N AB of species B about two different particles i and j of species A. In addition to driving the sampling of new compositions, the external potential also increases the correlations between particles in the system. One situation where the covariance term is expected to be large is in near-critical systems. Although our system is not under critical conditions, the observation of a significant and positive covariance contribution strongly indicates correlated fluctuations in the distribution of local densities. Our results indicate that decreasing the period length actually increases the over-estimation of the A–B interaction parameter Δucg . Examining the statistics [Fig. 6(b)] for the local densities N AB clarifies this situation: although increasing the number of periods (increasing 1/Lperiod ) indeed increases the average local density {NAB } and total number of A–B contacts, we find that it actually decreases the total variance of total A-B contacts and thus decreases the Fisher information. The interpretation is clear: if the period length of the external potential is too small, the effective chemical environment becomes “homogeneous” in the sense that every particle sees the same interfacial environment. B. Methanol–water Finally, we consider coarse graining from an atomistic model of methanol–water mixtures to single-site, Gaussian-repulsive beads. There is one minor distinction that needs to be made between the coarse-graining procedure of the atomistic model and that of the model symmetric binary mixture described in Sec. III A. Due to

Fortunately, we are primarily interested in the mixture thermodynamics, which involve composition variations at constant pressure. Taking inspiration from incompressible models72,87 and dissipative particle dynamics models58,88 of the thermodynamics of liquid mixtures, we similarly posit that accurate mixing thermodynamics can be achieved as long as the coarse-grained model is sufficiently incompressible. The coarse-grain pressure should be immaterial, as long as the coarse-grained model predicts molecule densities that are comparable to the atomistic ones, and activity coefficients are evaluated at the same constant pressure. Given this justification, we choose the coarse-grained pressure for the water–methanol system such that the coarse-grained water, at the atomistic water density, has a compressibility matching that of the atomistic water model (4.55 × 10−10 Pa−1 ). IV. DISCUSSION There is growing evidence in the literature that the choice of the simulation ensemble used for coarse graining can have significant effects upon the resulting coarse-grained models.39,56,64,65,90 This study builds upon these observations by asking if it is possible to systematically optimize the coarse-graining ensemble. The presented results show that choosing an external potential by maximizing the second derivative of the relative entropy gives the most accurate activity coefficients, emphasizing the important role of the second derivative of the relative entropy in the design and optimization of ensembles. This observation can be both statistically and physically rationalized: in this work, the second derivative of the relative entropy can be identified with both the Fisher information and the variance of structural variables [Eq. (2.15)]. This correspondence indicates that the most “informative” ensembles maximize variances in structural variables and sample a wider distribution of possible configurations, reminiscent of the flat histogram and enhanced sampling approaches.31,32,91–93 This enhanced sampling, in turn, helps the coarse-graining process to better assess the performance of different parameterizations over a broader variety of structural configurations and is found to correlate with improved thermodynamic faithfulness. In this work, the relevant structural variable is the total number of Gaussian-weighted pair A–B contacts [Eq. (2.17)] or the sum of local concentrations N AB,i of species B about particle i of species A [Eq. (2.19)]. The Fisher information of this structural variable has

[Page 14]

a rich dependence on the choice of ensemble, with some counterintuitive implications for the design of the ensemble. For example, a natural attempt to improve the sampling of A–B contacts is to try an extended ensemble approach. In this work, the five-state extended ensemble actually gave the highest overestimates of activity coefficients. This is surprising because sampling from five different mixtures (ranging from xB = 0.1 to xB = 0.9) covers a wide range of possible compositions. However, due to the fixed weighting and independence of the sub-ensembles, the Fisher information [Eq. (2.16)] indicates that the statistically appropriate way to measure the information content of the fixed-weight extended ensemble is as a weighted average of the variances of each individual system in the extended ensemble, rather than as a variance over all compositions included in the extended ensemble. Alternatively, one might seek to enhance the sampling of A–B contacts by increasing the number of periods of the external potential. However, the numerical results indicate that while this strategy increases the number of interfaces and A–B contacts, it actually decreases the variance of A–B contacts and hence the Fisher information. Instead, we observe that the Fisher information is maximized for potentials with a single period and intermediate amplitudes. This maximum is driven predominantly by correlations σij2 between the local concentrations N AB,i and N AB,j seen by two particles i, j rather than simply the variance σii2 of possible environments seen by a single particle. In contrast to the fixed-weight extended ensemble, the optimized external potential promotes collective, correlated fluctuations that significantly increase the perparticle Fisher information and improve the reproduction of activity coefficients. In essence, our approach leverages forcings to enhance the sampling of composition variations and fluctuations. This idea that reproducing a system’s response to perturbations leads to accurate thermodynamic models should be intuitive, and similar ideas have been applied to the top-down parameterization of the coarsegrained models. For example, Anderson et al. parameterized coarsegrained dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) models by calibrating them to reproduce the experimental partition coefficients.54 Our work demonstrates that the idea of matching perturbation responses can be applied equally fruitfully to achieve thermodynamically faithful bottom-up coarse graining. Our observation that low values of the second derivative of the relative entropy correlate with thermodynamically inaccurate coarse-grained models indicates flat optimization landscapes that fail to discriminate between the thermodynamic fidelity of models. This closely echoes long-standing observation in the literature of very shallow coarse-graining functional landscapes,4 and force-matching and structure-matching approaches can produce non-unique optimized potentials. For example, Rosenberger et al.8 systematically tuned convergence parameters and investigated the ability of structure-matching approaches to reproduce exactly the original pairwise interaction potential of a Lennard-Jones mixture even when no degrees of freedom are eliminated. In this case, there are theoretical guarantees that structure-matching approaches are optimized by the original pairwise potential.24,94 Nevertheless, despite reproducing pair distribution functions within numerical tolerances, the resulting optimized potentials varied widely and predicted values of the pressure that differed by multiple orders of magnitude. Similar results were observed recently in the
J. Chem. Phys. 153, 154116 (2020); doi: 10.1063/5.0022808 Published under license by AIP Publishing

context of coarse graining using both force and structure matching coarse-graining methods.44 The relative entropy analysis in the present study suggests that an information-theoretic interpretation is broadly applicable across coarse-graining approaches. The frequently observed shallowness of coarse graining in a single-state NVT ensemble (in both force and structure matching studies) corresponds to a low Fisher information, and in this sense, such an ensemble is “information poor.” Both in this work and in the literature, these informationpoor ensembles lead to coarse-grained models with unreliable thermodynamic properties. On the other hand, we expect that the “information rich” ensembles considered in this work, with steeper information-theoretic landscapes as characterized by the relative entropy, should also be applicable to improving thermodynamic faithfulness when coarse graining using force matching and structure matching methods. It is also interesting to revisit the previous studies in the literature that have successfully developed transferable coarse-grained models through the judicious choice of physically motivated ensembles. One notable approach to building coarse-grained models is a non-iterative method where the conditional reversible work (CRW) to turn off the interactions between two moeities at a fixed distance r is directly used as the coarse-grained pair potential.95,96 Although the non-iterative nature of the CRW approach sacrifices structural accuracy, in some cases, the CRW potential has been shown to be thermodynamically more transferable than common iterative approaches such as iterative Monte Carlo.97 In essence, the CRW leverages carefully selected pairs of ensembles: the fully interacting ensemble and the conditional ensemble where pairs of interactions are turned off. This conditional ensemble can, in turn, be interpreted as an external-force biased ensemble, and the CRW can be interpreted as making effective use of an “extended ensemble” of intelligently biased ensembles. The CRW points to further exploring ensembles where the biasing potential is applied between particles, in addition to the one-body potentials considered in this work. Another insightful study showed that iterative Boltzmann inversion on a supercooled liquid crystal system improved the coarse-grained model predictions of an isotropic–smectic transition.90 This study carefully discusses its choice of coarse-graining ensemble in the context of transferability of chemical and bonding environments.95,98 Most importantly, this study drew connections to observations in classical density functional theories that, at suitable density and temperature, the short-ranged order in the ordered phase is not very different from that in the disordered phase.99 Carefully finding such state points to coarse grain from was then shown to improve the resulting coarse-grained models of liquid crystals.90 Perhaps the supercooled ensemble employed in this study can be shown to exhibit higher Fisher information—if so, then one can imagine optimizing the degree of supercooling employed during coarse graining. Finally, we address some outstanding questions that need to be answered to fully generalize the observations and methods proposed in this work. Although we have observed strong correlations between the magnitude of the Fisher information and the thermodynamic faithfulness of the coarse-grained models, away from the Fisher information maximum, we have also observed that the relationship may not be monotonic. In general, this is not surprising

[Page 15]

because we expect the Fisher information to also encode structural information alongside thermodynamic information. To disentangle these contributions, we have conducted work partitioning the Fisher information into thermodynamic and “additional” (mostly structural) contributions. This analysis will be presented in future studies. Another important question to address is the proper interpretation of the Fisher information in the multi-parameter situation, where the Fisher information will be a matrix instead of a scalar. While the Fisher information matrix will offer a rich description of the parameter space, it is likely that collapsing it to a single scalar (e.g., by taking the determinant) may not be the best way to analyze and optimize the matrix, and the correlation with thermodynamic faithfulness may require multi-objective optimization techniques. Proper interpretation of the Fisher information matrix will be critical to sensibly optimizing coarse-graining ensembles.

V. CONCLUSION We have demonstrated that rationally choosing the ensemble can significantly improve the thermodynamic faithfulness of bottom-up coarse-grained models. This coarse-graining strategy is based on two central observations: (1) coarse graining should be conducted in ensembles where variables conjugate to the thermodynamic properties of interest are sampled over a broad range, possibly enhanced by strong forcings, and (2) measuring the second derivative of the relative entropy with respect to coarse-grained parameters allows one to quantify and optimize the “informativeness” of simulation ensembles. In practice, this means we coarse grain pure fluids from the NPT ensemble and cross-interactions in binary mixtures from ensembles with applied external potentials. Then, by maximizing the second derivative of the relative entropy, external potential ensembles are constructed whose coarse-grained models faithfully reproduce activity coefficients across the entire composition range, even for highly simplified coarse-grained interaction models. This coarse-graining approach highlights and leverages connections between statistical measures of information, enhanced sampling, and improved coarse-grained models. The technique is computationally efficient, requiring only a handful of simulations and avoids costly chemical potential measurements. The literature features extensive work on increasing the complexity of the coarsegrained interaction to improve the parameterization and performance of coarse-grained models. In contrast, the success of the simple Gaussian repulsive potentials considered in this work underscores the importance of the ensemble as an orthogonal handle to achieve the same goal. We expect that similar improvements will be observed if the same ideas are applied to parameterize more complex interaction potentials. We believe that our work offers a promising new avenue in the design of maximally informative ensembles, enabling the learning of accurate coarse-grained models with less data. Limited data are a bottleneck in the computational learning of accurate models, putting a premium on the efficient generation and use of data.35,100 The Fisher information metric can guide the high-dimensional parameterization of multicomponent systems by identifying low Fisher information coarse-grained parameters that are only weakly constrained by the coarse-graining data; computational resources

J. Chem. Phys. 153, 154116 (2020); doi: 10.1063/5.0022808 Published under license by AIP Publishing

should then be allocated to obtaining data capable of resolving these parameters. This is critical, for example, in large scale, automated parameterizations of coarse-grained models from atomistic force fields.
